skill.name: xdrop
skill.description: Upload local files or directories to an Xdrop server, create encrypted share links, and download Xdrop links by decrypting files locally.
skill.summary: Send and fetch encrypted files through an Xdrop server from the terminal.
skill.author: xixu-me

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, network, environment, filesystem, and crypto patterns. Review found no shell execution or prompt injection, and the crypto findings are mostly false positives because the scripts use WebCrypto AES-GCM, HKDF with SHA-256, and random IV material. The remaining risk is medium because the skill intentionally reads local files, sends encrypted content to user-selected Xdrop endpoints, and writes downloaded files to disk.

medium_findings.title: Intentional Network Upload Of Local Files
medium_findings.description: The upload workflow reads user-selected files and sends encrypted chunks and metadata to an Xdrop server selected by --server or --api-url. This is expected behavior, but it can disclose sensitive files if an agent uploads the wrong path or uses an untrusted endpoint.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The script clearly reads local input paths and performs PUT and API fetch requests to the configured Xdrop service. This is legitimate for the skill, but it is a real data disclosure risk if invoked on sensitive paths.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Download Writes Files To Disk
medium_findings.description: The download workflow fetches a remote manifest and chunks, decrypts them, and writes files under the requested output directory. Path segments are sanitized, which reduces traversal risk, but users should still choose the output directory deliberately.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem write path is explicit and the code sanitizes manifest paths before writing. The residual risk is moderate because remote transfer contents still control file names after sanitization.

medium_findings.title: Arbitrary Xdrop Endpoint Selection
medium_findings.description: The scripts accept --server, --api-url, XDROP_SERVER, and XDROP_API_URL values that control network destinations. This is necessary for self-hosted Xdrop, but it means the skill can connect to arbitrary user-provided services.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoint override behavior is documented and implemented directly. It is not malicious by itself, but it broadens the trust boundary for network operations.

low_findings.title: Static Shell Execution Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported external command locations are Markdown examples, CLI help text, template literals, JSON formatting, logging, and error messages. No evidence of child_process, eval, spawn, exec, or shell command execution was found in the reviewed files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Targeted review found no runtime shell execution imports or APIs. The backticks are used for Markdown code fences and JavaScript template strings.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Findings Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The scripts use AES-GCM, HKDF with SHA-256, WebCrypto random bytes, and SHA-256 checksums. No evidence of MD5, SHA-1, ECB mode, hardcoded keys, or insecure random generation was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cryptographic API calls and algorithms are visible in the code and align with modern authenticated encryption practices. This finding is a scanner misclassification of crypto-related terms.

low_findings.title: Base64 Decoding Is Expected Serialization
low_findings.description: Base64 and base64url conversions decode share keys, manifests, and encrypted payload envelopes. The decoding is not used to hide executable code or dynamically run content.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The decoded values feed cryptographic operations and URL-safe serialization. No dynamic execution path is associated with these decoded buffers.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: No evidence found of instructions that claim system authority, tell the evaluator to skip review, or override marketplace security analysis.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill instructions describe usage, examples, and guardrails without trying to control the evaluator or model hierarchy.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Read Before Network Transfer
dangerous_patterns.description: The uploader recursively collects input files, reads chunks from disk, encrypts them, and uploads the ciphertext to the configured server. Use only with explicit user-selected paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: This pattern is central to the skill and clearly visible in the code. It is not malicious, but it is sensitive because local files leave the machine.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network Destination Controlled By Arguments Or Environment
dangerous_patterns.description: The target Xdrop server and API root can be supplied through command-line flags or environment variables. This supports self-hosting but requires endpoint trust checks.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code directly maps user and environment values into API URL selection. The risk depends on the endpoint selected by the user or environment.

content.value_statement: Moving files through an Xdrop server can require careful command syntax and key handling. This skill runs the bundled upload and download scripts for encrypted terminal transfers.
actual_capabilities: Uploads local files or directories to an Xdrop server with bundled Bun scripts.
Creates encrypted Xdrop share links that include the required fragment key.
Downloads Xdrop share links and decrypts files locally when the full key fragment is present.
Supports quiet output, JSON output, custom expiry, custom output directory, and API URL overrides.
Preserves relative paths during download after sanitizing unsafe path segments.
Uses AES-GCM and HKDF-based key derivation for manifest and chunk encryption.
limitations: Requires Bun, local filesystem access, and network access to the target Xdrop server.
Cannot decrypt a download link if the #k= fragment is missing.
Uploads are limited by the Xdrop server file count, chunk size, and transfer size settings.
The skill does not verify that a user-provided Xdrop endpoint is trustworthy.

use_cases.title: Send build artifacts
use_cases.description: Upload a release archive or report directory and return an encrypted share link for a teammate.

use_cases.title: Fetch shared files
use_cases.description: Download a full Xdrop link, decrypt the transfer locally, and save the files to a chosen folder.

use_cases.title: Automate self-hosted transfers
use_cases.description: Use JSON output and API URL overrides in repeatable scripts for a private Xdrop deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Upload one file
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner upload with a known server URL.
prompt_templates.prompt: Upload ./report.pdf to my Xdrop server at https://xdrop.example.com and give me the share link.

prompt_templates.title: Download a share link
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner download to a specific folder.
prompt_templates.prompt: Download this Xdrop link to ./downloads and decrypt it locally: https://xdrop.example.com/t/abc123#k=KEY

prompt_templates.title: Upload with machine-readable output
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate automation with expiry and JSON output.
prompt_templates.prompt: Upload ./dist with a 10 minute expiry, use quiet JSON output, and return the transfer ID and share URL.

prompt_templates.title: Use a custom API root
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced self-hosted deployment with separate public and API URLs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Upload ./archive.zip through https://files.example.com, but use https://api.example.com/api/v1 as the Xdrop API endpoint.

output_examples.input: Upload a single PDF to an Xdrop server.
output_examples.output: The assistant returns an encrypted Xdrop share link and notes the expiry reported by the server.

output_examples.input: Download a full Xdrop link into ./downloads.
output_examples.output: The assistant reports the destination folder and lists the saved files in plain language.

output_examples.input: Upload a folder with quiet JSON output.
output_examples.output: The assistant summarizes the transfer ID, share URL, and expiration time without exposing internal command logs.

best_practices: Confirm the exact files or directories before uploading content to any Xdrop server.
Use trusted HTTPS Xdrop endpoints unless a local development server is intended.
Keep the full #k= fragment private because it is required to decrypt downloads.
anti_patterns: Do not upload broad home directories, secret folders, or repository roots without review.
Do not use an unknown --api-url or XDROP_API_URL value for sensitive files.
Do not remove the fragment key from a share link before asking for a download.

faq.question: What does this skill do?
faq.answer: It uploads files to Xdrop, creates encrypted share links, and downloads full Xdrop links for local decryption.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it need network access?
faq.answer: Yes. It must connect to the selected Xdrop server and to server-provided upload or download URLs.

faq.question: Does it read local files?
faq.answer: Yes. Uploads read the files or directories named by the user before encrypting and sending chunks.

faq.question: Can it download without the #k= fragment?
faq.answer: No. The fragment contains key material needed to decrypt the transfer locally.

faq.question: Is it safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a medium-risk warning because file upload and arbitrary endpoint selection require user care.
